rationale: The page provides a comprehensive overview of online gaming regulations in India, including the classification of games and legal penalties. It also details the National Sports Governance Act, 2025, covering the constitutional jurisdiction of sports and the establishment of national sports bodies.
cleanedText:
Navigating India's Online Gaming Regulations
The New Gaming Landscape: Online Games Now Split into Three Categories
- Officially Recognized as a Legitimate Sport: Ministry of Youth Affairs & Sports will frame guidelines to promote it.
- Online Social Games.
- Online Money Games.
Rules & Consequences
- Absolute Ban on Online Money Games: Prohibits real-money games, their ads, and related financial transactions.
- Severe Penalties for Offering Money Games: Up to 3 years imprisonment plus a fine of ₹1 crore.
- A National Online Gaming Authority is Being Formed: It will classify games, register platforms, and handle grievances.
- Regulations Apply to All Games Targeting Indian Users: Includes services offered within India or operated from outside the country.
Other Initiatives to Counter Risks of Online Gaming
- Section 69A of IT Act, 2000: Empowers Government to block access to illegal websites. E.g., 1,524 betting and gambling websites and mobile apps blocked during 2022-25.
- IT Rules, 2021: Provide norms for online gaming platforms. E.g. Registration of Intermediaries with Self-regulatory bodies.
- Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023: Section 111 and 112 penalise unlawful economic activities and cybercrimes and unauthorised betting and gambling respectively.
- Consumer Protection Act, 2019: Prohibits misleading advertisements.
7.4. NATIONAL SPORTS GOVERNANCE ACT, 2025
Why in the news?
Recently, the National Sports Governance Act, 2025 came into force.
Sports governance in India
- Sports regulation falls within jurisdiction of states, as per Entry 33 of State List of Seventh Schedule.
- Existing policies include National Sports Policy (NSP) (1984, revised 2001) and Sports Broadcasting Signals (Mandatory Sharing with Prasar Bharati) Act, 2007.
- Recently, Cabinet approved NSP 2025, superseding the 2001 policy.
- It is structured around five core pillars, focusing on global excellence, economic and social development through sports, making sports a people movement, and integrating sports with education.
Key highlights of Act
- Aspect: Establish National Sports Bodies
- Detail:
- Includes National Olympic Committee, National Paralympic Committee, and National and Regional Sports Federations for each designated sport.
- Affiliated with international bodies and will have affiliated units at state and district levels.
